label: B1 — Intermediate
blurb: 4 partes, 25 preguntas: imágenes, opción múltiple, completar y entrevista.

audios.title: Part 1 · Questions 1–7
audios.intro: For each question, choose the correct answer (A, B or C). You will hear seven short recordings. Use the arrow ▶ or the numbers below to move on.
scripts: Question one. Mum: Have you finished packing for Ireland, Sofia? Sofia: Nearly. I've packed my camera and a good book for the plane. Mum: Put your umbrella in too — it rains there almost every day. Sofia: True! I'll get it from the hall.

Question two. Boy: Dad, is the airport bus early enough for my flight on Sunday? Dad: The first bus would get you there too late, Noah, so I'll drive you myself. Boy: Thanks, Dad. Can we leave at six?

Question three. Girl: What's the weather like in Montreal, Hugo? You arrive in January, right? Hugo: Yes — my host family says the city is completely white then. Snow every week! Girl: Wow, pack a warm coat!

Question four. Woman: What time is your flight tomorrow? Man: Well, we board at half past eight, but the plane actually takes off at nine. Woman: So we should be at the airport by seven, to be safe. Man: Exactly.

Question five. Girl: I want to give my host family something from home, Grandma. Maybe a cake? Woman: A cake might not survive the journey, Ruby. Why not a book with photos of our city? Ruby: Perfect — they can keep it forever.

Question six. Boy: Where do we go on the first morning, Elena? Straight to the classrooms? Elena: No — the message says all new students meet in the library at nine, for a welcome talk. Boy: The library. OK, I'll find it on the map.

Question seven. Woman: You look worried — what's wrong? Man: I can't find my bag! I've got my suitcase, and my phone's in my hand... Woman: Isn't that it, on the seat by the café? Man: Oh, thank goodness. All my documents are in there.

questions.q: What does Mum tell Sofia to pack?
How will Noah get to the airport?
What is the weather like in Montreal in January?
What time does the plane take off?
What will Ruby give her host family?
Where do new students meet on the first morning?
What has the man lost at the airport?

audios.title: Part 2 · Questions 8–13
audios.intro: For each question, choose the correct answer (A, B or C). You will hear six short recordings.
scripts: You will hear a girl talking about her host family abroad. Girl: Before I arrived I was nervous, because their emails were so short and formal. But in person they're the warmest people — the mother cooks with me every evening, and now we can't stop chatting. You can't judge people from emails.

You will hear a boy talking about his first weeks abroad. Boy: Everyone warned me I'd miss my parents, but strangely that's been fine — we video call on Sundays. What I really miss is the food from home. I'd give anything for a proper plate of my grandmother's cooking.

You will hear a woman talking about learning a language abroad. Woman: I took classes every morning, and they helped, but my real progress came from joining a swimming club. Nobody there spoke my language, so I had no choice but to communicate. That's when I finally stopped translating in my head.

You will hear a boy talking about something that surprised him. Boy: I expected the lessons to be different, and the food, obviously. What I never imagined was that shops would close so early — at home you can buy things almost at midnight! The first week I nearly missed dinner twice.

You will hear a girl comparing schools. Girl: My school here starts later in the morning, which I love. But the homework is another story — there's much more of it than at home, and teachers check it every single day. So it's not easier, just different.

You will hear a man giving advice to students going abroad. Man: Students always ask me which dictionary to buy or which apps to download. Honestly, those things matter less than one simple habit: say yes to every invitation in your first month. Every trip, every party, every game. That's how a foreign city becomes home.

questions.q: What does the girl say about her host family?
o: They are friendlier than they seemed
They are quite formal at home

questions.q: What does the boy miss most?
What helped the woman most with the language?
What does the girl say about her new school?
o: It is easier than her old school

questions.q: What is the man's main advice?

audios.title: Part 3 · Questions 14–19
audios.intro: You will hear a woman giving information to a group of exchange students. For each question, write the correct answer in the gap. Write one or two words, or a number, a date or a time.
scripts: Woman: Welcome, everyone, to your exchange programme. Let me quickly explain this week's arrangements. Your orientation meeting is on Wednesday — please don't miss it, because you'll receive your student cards there. The meeting is not at the school itself; it takes place in the town hall, opposite the train station. We start at ten o'clock sharp, and there'll be coffee and snacks afterwards. There are twenty-five students in this year's group, from eight different countries, so you'll make friends quickly. On Saturday we've organised a trip to the mountains, which costs fifteen pounds, including lunch — bring that money on Wednesday, please. And one essential thing for the meeting itself: bring your passport, because we need it to complete your registration. See you on Wednesday!

questions.label: Number of students in the group: ____

audios.title: Part 4 · Questions 20–25
audios.intro: You will hear an interview with a girl called Elena, who spent six months studying abroad. For each question, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).
scripts: Interviewer: My guest today is Elena, who has just come back from six months studying abroad. Elena, why did you decide to go? Elena: Most people go to improve the language, and of course I wanted that too. But my real reason was different — I'd lived in the same small town all my life, and I wanted to find out who I was somewhere nobody knew me. Interviewer: What was the hardest moment? Elena: Everyone expects me to say the first week, but that was actually exciting. The difficult time came after about two months, when the novelty disappeared and everyday problems began — bank letters, bus strikes, a broken washing machine. That's when I nearly gave up. Interviewer: But you didn't. What kept you there? Elena: My classmates, definitely. When I told them I was thinking of going home, they organised a weekend trip to the coast to cheer me up. After that, I never thought about leaving again. Interviewer: How was the school itself? Elena: The teachers were excellent, though the style took some getting used to. At home the teacher explains and we write. There, you're expected to argue and disagree — politely, of course. At first I found that shocking, later I loved it. Interviewer: What do you miss now that you're back? Elena: Funny you ask — not the famous sights at all. I miss my Tuesday evenings at the little cinema club, watching old films with the same ten people. Interviewer: Would you recommend studying abroad? Elena: Yes, but not to copy anyone else's experience. Go and make your own mistakes — they teach you more than the classes do.

questions.q: Why did Elena mainly want to study abroad?
o: To discover herself in a new place

questions.q: When was the most difficult time for Elena?
o: At the very end of her stay

questions.q: What made Elena decide to stay?
o: A trip organised by her classmates
A phone call from her family

questions.q: What surprised Elena about the lessons?
o: There was a lot of writing

questions.q: What does Elena miss most now?
What is Elena's advice to other students?
